.. _language_introduction:

Introduction
************

	In most applications there is a need to send arguments to a program in order
	to specify parameterised features which are required by the user. There are
	different ways of doing so, program arguments, environment variables and other
	means. The Language module implements utilities to specify program behaviour
	through **directives**. Directives are basically Python dictionaries, or can
	be turned into more advanced objects which are specified by a user either
	manually, through a user interface either GUI or web, or through process
	communication. A directive also includes a verb or an action to be taken.

	If you think of a data retrieval system from an archive, you can specify
	data to be retrieved through a 'retrieve' directive::

		retrieve(
			date = 2010010100,
			step = [12,24,48,60,72],
			parameter = "geopotential",
			level = [1000,500],
			version = "oper",
			etc...
		)

	The same data could be used to request the archive to first stage the data::

		stage(
			date = 2010010100,
			step = [12,24,48,60,72],
			parameter = "geopotential",
			level = [1000,500],
			version = "oper",
			etc...
		)

	The Language utilities check that the keywords used are valid, that if some keywords
	are compulsary, they are provided, sets some default values, convert data to the right
	type or class etc... That way we can make sure that the data provided to objects and
	functions are valid and clean, no more tests are required.
